nodes.label: sanad-cases.png
nodes.summary: UI screenshot of the Sanad Case Board — a Kanban-style view displaying 6 active legal cases across 4 workflow stages (Drafting, Client Review, Filed, Closed). Each case card shows client name, priority tag (Normal/High/Urgent/Low), case-type tag (contract, ip, litigation, corporate, consultation), due date with countdown, and SAR billing value. The app is described as targeting Saudi legal and SME professionals; footer notes 'Home-server optimized · PWA enabled'.

nodes.label: CasesView / Case Board
nodes.summary: The primary case-management view in Sanad, rendered as a drag-and-drop Kanban board with four pipeline stages. Hosts a '+ New case' action button and shows aggregate counts per column. Active case count (6) displayed in subtitle.

nodes.label: Case Workflow Stages
nodes.summary: Four sequential pipeline stages for legal case progression visible in the board: Drafting (2), Client Review (2), Filed (2), Closed (1). Colour-coded column headers indicate stage category. Cases may be dragged across stages.

nodes.label: Case Entity / Data Model
nodes.summary: A legal case record rendered on each card with: title, client name, priority level (Normal / High / Urgent / Low), one or more type tags (contract, ip, litigation, corporate, consultation), ISO due date, optional countdown (e.g. '5d left', '3d left'), and a monetary value in SAR (e.g. 1,200,000 SAR). Some cases show 0 value, indicating unbilled or pro-bono matters.

nodes.label: Sanad Sidebar Navigation
nodes.summary: Left sidebar navigation visible in this screenshot with items: Dashboard, Compliance (badge: 4), Case Board (active/highlighted), Deep Work, Tasks (badge: 9), Documents. Badge counts surface pending compliance items and open tasks. Bottom sidebar shows PWA install hints: 'PWA-enabled. Install to desktop for daily access.' and 'Timer & UI state cached locally — minimal server load.'

nodes.label: PWA / Home-Server Design
nodes.summary: The app communicates its PWA posture in two places: sidebar hints ('PWA-enabled. Install to desktop for daily access.' / 'Timer & UI state cached locally — minimal server load.') and the footer ('Home-server optimized · PWA enabled'). Signals offline-capable, locally-cached UI state, and desktop-installability as design goals.
